content.assessment: This proposal reconstructs a bargaining relationship that Hungary's 2013 and 2017 centralization explicitly dismantled (F-live-portuguese_reform-01), so its central premise requires reversing part of the very structural reform that created current conditions. The lens question of whether non-structural or quasi-contractual measures can substitute for structural authority is directly engaged: without restored municipal fiscal or legal standing, a contract-style mechanism risks being symbolic. No evidence supplied addresses whether such reciprocal arrangements would counteract the disadvantage concentration or church/state asymmetry documented elsewhere (F-live-portuguese_reform-03, F-live-portuguese_reform-08).
opportunities: Could serve as one component of a larger coordinated bundle if paired with structural funding guarantees, consistent with the lens's emphasis on necessary coordinated reforms rather than isolated fixes
strengths: Explicitly acknowledges that Hungary's centralized structure requires a state-initiated rather than municipal-right-based contract, showing awareness of structural constraints (F-live-portuguese_reform-01)
threats: Risk that a symbolic contract is credited with outcomes actually driven by unrelated demographic or funding shifts, violating the causal-attribution caution central to this lens
weaknesses: Municipal bargaining power was structurally removed, and no mechanism restores the fiscal capacity needed to make reciprocity credible (F-live-portuguese_reform-01)

No evidence provided on how contracts would interact with quality-levelling problems like teacher shortages or church-school selection freedom (F-live-portuguese_reform-04, F-live-portuguese_reform-08)
